ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class (FOODON_03301099); no specific passionfruit-flavoured green tea entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea-based beverages broadly; no passionfruit green tea sub-code exists. Closest parent node used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for passionfruit green tea beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one regular-sized cup as commonly sold at Singaporean beverage stalls and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Passionfruit Green Tea — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Passionfruit Green Tea (cr_id D08010080). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for passionfruit green tea. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage derives its glycaemic impact primarily from sucrose/fructose added sugar at 9.54g/100g. Sucrose GI is approximately 65; dilution in a tea matrix and minor fibre content moderates this slightly to an estimated GI of 60. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (60 × 12.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — added sugar at 9.54g/100g drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, both of which independently elevate blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; request an unsweetened or zero-sugar version, as added sugar in beverages independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content (1 mg/100g), consistent with AHA 2018 guidance on SSBs and hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 9.54g sugar/100g with negligible dietary fibre (0.03g/100g) and medium GI (~60) causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; liquid sugar absorbs faster than equivalent solid-food carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; even 'less sugar' variants remain unsuitable as SSBs, and the green tea polyphenols (EGCG) cannot offset the acute glycaemic impact of 23.9g of sugar per 250 ml serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar content (9.54g/100g) in a liquid vehicle promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and visceral adiposity; however, fat and sodium are negligible and green tea polyphenols confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage in its sweetened form due to the added sugar load promoting dyslipidaemia; switching to an unsweetened green tea version would retain the cardioprotective EGCG polyphenols without the adverse sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.54g/100g) with negligible fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; green tea EGCG polyphenols are anti-inflammatory but insufficient to offset the sugar-driven inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to an unsweetened passionfruit green tea to benefit from EGCG anti-inflammatory polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory added sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Passionfruit Green Tea is a popular sugar-sweetened cold or iced beverage widely sold at Singaporean bubble tea shops, café chains, and hawker beverage stalls. It is prepared by brewing green tea — typically a light-bodied variety such as jasmine green or sencha — and combining it with passionfruit flavouring syrup or concentrate, resulting in a refreshing drink with a floral, tropical-tart taste and a mildly astringent green tea finish. The beverage is customisable with toppings such as pearls, jelly, or aloe vera. Per 100 ml, it provides 48 kcal, 12.44g carbohydrate, 9.54g sugar, 0.01g protein, and 0.0g fat, making it a high-sugar, low-nutrient-density drink. The negligible dietary fibre (0.03g/100g) and low sodium (1 mg/100g) do not offset the glycaemic impact of the added sugar. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. Green tea polyphenols (EGCG) provide modest antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits but cannot compensate for the sugar load. An unsweetened version would be significantly more health-appropriate. The estimated glycemic index is 60 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 18.7 per 250 ml serving.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink passionfruit green tea?
Is passionfruit green tea suitable for hypertension?
Green tea with passionfruit flavour Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010080) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source for Singapore beverages. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to sugar-sweetened tea beverages, which slightly reduces overall confidence for the GI component only; all other values are exact-source.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010080 — this is a sugar-sweetened tea beverage containing no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation as no fat-containing ingredients are present. protein_g is 0.01g/100g (trace level), not zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific passionfruit green tea beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010080 as of 2026. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for passionfruit green tea was found in the approved GI source database. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 31.1) / 100 = 18.7.
